reasoning.step: Define "oxygen-annealed specimen" as a sample that, after the main high-temperature synthesis steps, was subjected to a final heat treatment in flowing oxygen at $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for 5 hours; this is the sample on which the magnetic measurements discussed below were performed.

Define "SQUID magnetometer" as the superconducting-quantum-interference-device magnetometer (Quantum Design) used to measure magnetic susceptibility $\chi(T)$ as a function of temperature $T$ in an externally applied magnetic field $H$; the applied field used here is $H=27\,$Oe, where $1\ \mathrm{Oe}$ denotes one oersted.

Define "zero-field-cooled (ZFC) susceptibility" as the measured magnetic susceptibility obtained when the specimen is cooled to low temperature in zero applied field and then the external field $H$ is applied before measuring $\chi(T)$ on warming; define "field-cooled (FC) susceptibility" as the susceptibility measured while cooling the specimen in the presence of the applied field $H$.

State the observation that in the ZFC measurement the low-temperature susceptibility $\chi(T=6\ \mathrm{K})$ attains approximately $100\%$ of the ideal superconducting screening value $1/4\pi$ (where $1/4\pi$ denotes the ideal demagnetizing-free susceptibility of a perfectly diamagnetic superconductor in cgs units), under the assumption of an average mass density $\rho\approx6\ \mathrm{g\,cm^{-3}}$ for the specimen; this is reported as indicating complete magnetic screening of the sample at $T=6\ \mathrm{K}$. Fig. 2

Define the assumed mass density symbol $\rho$ as $\rho\approx6\ \mathrm{g\,cm^{-3}}$, the value the authors used to convert measured magnetization into an approximate superconducting-volume fraction; note that this assumption is explicitly invoked by the authors rather than derived.

Report the observation that the FC susceptibility reaches roughly $10\%$ of the maximum possible value at low temperature (i.e., $\sim10\%$ of $1/4\pi$) for the same measurement, and interpret this as a lower-bound estimate of the true superconducting volume fraction in the sample because field-cooling measurements can underestimate the screened fraction compared with ZFC measurements. Fig. 2

Report the observation that the diamagnetic onset temperature (the temperature where $\chi(T)$ first departs from the normal-state value toward diamagnetism) is $T_{c}\approx133.5\,$K, seen in both ZFC and FC measurements; record $T_{c}$ as the onset temperature of superconducting diamagnetism in this oxygen-annealed specimen. Fig. 2

State the additional FC-data observation that the FC susceptibility reaches approximately $60\%$ of its full low-temperature FC value by $T=125\,$K, which the authors take as "strongly indicating" that the phase with $T_{c}\approx133.5\,$K dominates the superconducting response near 125 K. Fig. 2

Report the ZFC-curve observation of subsidiary diamagnetic features (additional decreases in $\chi(T)$) at $T\approx126\,$K and at $T\approx112\,$K; define these features as distinct steps or shoulders in the ZFC susceptibility curve and record the authors' interpretation that these indicate the presence of several distinct superconducting phases within the multiphase specimen. Fig. 2

Explicitly record that the authors treat the magnetic evidence (the high onset $T_{c}\approx133.5\,$K, the full ZFC screening at low temperature under the stated density assumption, the partial FC shielding fraction, and the additional ZFC features) as demonstrating bulk superconducting diamagnetism in the oxygen-annealed HBCCO specimen, while also acknowledging that multiphase behavior is present. Fig. 2

conclusion.title: Magnetic detection of superconductivity with onset near 133.5 K and multi-phase magnetic signature

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: The oxygen-annealed HBCCO specimen exhibits superconducting diamagnetism with an onset temperature $T_{c}\approx133.5\,$K measured in both zero-field-cooled (ZFC) and field-cooled (FC) SQUID magnetometry in an external field $H=27\,$Oe. The ZFC susceptibility at $T=6\,$K attains approximately $100\%$ of the ideal superconducting screening value $1/4\pi$ (assuming an average mass density $\rho\approx6~\mathrm{g\,cm^{-3}}$), indicating full magnetic screening of the sample at low temperature; the FC susceptibility reaches roughly $10\%$ of the maximum possible value at low temperature and attains $\sim60\%$ of its full low-temperature value by $T=125\,$K. The ZFC curve also shows additional diamagnetic features at $126\,$K and $112\,$K, indicating the presence of several distinct superconducting phases within the specimen.

reasoning.step: Define the four-probe resistivity measurement as the temperature-dependent electrical resistance $R(T)$ measured with a standard four-probe a.c.-resistance bridge; the particular specimen discussed was an oxygen-annealed HBCCO pellet whose resistance was normalized to $R(300)\approx0.10\ \Omega$ for presentation. Fig. 3

State the observation that $R(T)$ exhibits a sharp drop starting at high temperature, with the temperature derivative $dR/dT$ showing a clear maximum at $T\approx132.5\,$K; the inset of the resistivity plot displays $dR/dT$ to make this maximum explicit. Fig. 3

Define the symbol $dR/dT$ as the temperature derivative of the resistance $R(T)$, and record that the maximum in $dR/dT$ is used by the authors to indicate the temperature where the resistive transition is most rapid, here at $T\approx132.5\,$K. Fig. 3

Report the experimental observation that $R(T)$ reaches exactly zero resistance (within the instrumental resolution of the four-probe a.c.-resistance bridge used) at $T=95\,$K; explicitly state that "zero resistance" here means "below the resolution limit of the bridge" as the authors do. Fig. 3

State the comparative observation that as-sintered (that is, not oxygen-annealed) samples showed a maximum observed $T_{c}$ of only $\sim117\,$K, and conclude (as the authors do) that the final oxygen annealing treatment was very effective in increasing the maximum observed transition temperature up to the present onset near $132.5$–$133.5\,$K. Fig. 3

Record the authors' combined transport conclusion: the resistivity data provide a high-temperature resistive anomaly with onset near $132.5\,$K (maximum $dR/dT$ there) and a zero-resistance state at $95\,$K in the annealed specimen, and oxygen annealing raised the observed onset temperature compared with as-sintered material. Fig. 3

conclusion.title: Electrical resistivity behavior: sharp drop near 132.5 K and zero resistance at 95 K; oxygen annealing raises onset temperature

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: The four-probe resistivity $R(T)$ of an annealed HBCCO specimen shows a sharp drop in $R$ with a maximum in $dR/dT$ at $T\approx132.5\,$K and achieves exactly zero resistance within instrumental resolution at $T=95\,$K. Comparison with as-sintered samples shows that the final oxygen annealing increased the maximum observed $T_{c}$ from $\sim117\,$K (as-sintered) to the present onset near $132.5$–$133.5\,$K (oxygen-annealed).

reasoning.step: Define the structural characterization methods used: X-ray diffraction by the Guinier technique (for bulk powder diffraction), energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX/EDS) for local elemental analysis, selected-area electron-diffraction (SAED) for local reciprocal-lattice information, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) for real-space imaging of lattice planes and stacking sequences; these techniques were applied to the oxygen-annealed black material and to individual plate-like grains extracted from it.

Report the EDX/EDS compositional observation that about $15\%$ of the total sample volume consisted of plate-like grains containing Hg, Ba, Ca and Cu; state that some of these plate-like grains were selected for detailed SAED and HRTEM analysis.

State the SAED/HRTEM structural observation that the plate-like Hg–Ba–Ca–Cu-containing grains "consist mostly of pure $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ (Hg-1223), disordered mixtures of Hg-1223 and $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$ (Hg-1212), and a periodic stacking sequence of the latter unit cells"; record that this is the authors' identification based on the electron-diffraction patterns and high-resolution imaging. Fig. 1

Explain that accurate bulk lattice-constant determination by X-ray Guinier diffraction was not possible because the volume fraction of the phases of interest is small; consequently, the authors deduced lattice-parameter values from SAED patterns instead.

State the numerical lattice-constant deductions from SAED: for the tetragonal Hg-1212 unit $c=12.7(2)\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ (where $\mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ denotes an angstrom), for the tetragonal Hg-1223 unit $c=16.1(3)\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$, and for both unit types $a=3.93(7)\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$; record that the parenthetical uncertainties are those reported by the authors.

Describe the HRTEM image-based observation that a representative grain in $[100]$ orientation shows a stacking sequence (given by the authors as example) 1223/1223/1212/1212/1223/1213, which produces a supercell with $c\approx86.4\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ and that this stacking extends beyond $2{,}000\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$, thereby qualifying the superstructure as a distinct phase rather than a finite-size faulted region. Fig. 1

Record the negative structural observation that both HRTEM images and SAED patterns gave no evidence for the presence of Hg-1201-type grains or for HgO double layers in the examined grains; treat this absence as an explicit experimental observation reported by the authors. Fig. 1

Summarize the authors' structural conclusion: the plate-like grains analyzed by SAED and HRTEM are identified predominantly as Hg-1223, as disordered Hg-1223/Hg-1212 mixtures, and as an ordered long-period superstructure of these unit cells, and numerical lattice constants $c=12.7(2)\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ (Hg-1212), $c=16.1(3)\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ (Hg-1223), and $a=3.93(7)\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ (both) are deduced from the electron-diffraction data. Fig. 1

conclusion.title: Structural identification of Hg-1223, Hg-1212 and a long-period superstructure; lattice constants deduced

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and selected-area electron-diffraction (SAED) identify plate-like grains composed predominantly of $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ (Hg-1223), disordered mixtures of Hg-1223 and $HgBa_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{6+x}$ (Hg-1212), and an ordered periodic stacking (superstructure) of these unit cells. From SAED patterns the tetragonal lattice constants are deduced as $c=12.7(2)\,\mathrm{\AA}$ for Hg-1212, $c=16.1(3)\,\mathrm{\AA}$ for Hg-1223, and $a=3.93(7)\,\mathrm{\AA}$ for both unit types. HRTEM images show an example stacking sequence 1223/1223/1212/1212/1223/1213 with a supercell $c\approx86.4\,\mathrm{\AA}$ extending beyond $2000\,\mathrm{\AA}$, establishing the superstructure as a distinct phase. No Hg-1201-type grains or HgO double layers were detected in the examined grains.

reasoning.step: Define energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX or EDS) as the local compositional analysis technique applied to the bulk annealed black material and to individual grains; also define "bulk characterization" to include X-ray Guinier diffraction as used by the authors.

State the quantitative EDX/bulk-composition findings reported by the authors for the annealed black material: approximately $30\%$ by volume of $BaCuO_{2}$ grains, approximately $30\%$ by volume of $CuO$ grains, roughly $15\%$ by volume of an unidentified oxide containing Hg, Ca and Cu, less than $5\%$ by volume of an oxide containing Ca and Cu, about $5\%$ of unspecified impurities, and about $15\%$ of the total volume consisting of plate-like Hg–Ba–Ca–Cu-containing grains (the grains that HRTEM/SAED associate with Hg-1223/Hg-1212).

Record that the authors used these EDX-derived volume-fraction estimates to emphasize that the sample is a multiphase composite and that only a minority fraction (about $15\%$) of the sample volume consists of the plate-like Hg-containing grains of interest for associating structure with superconductivity.

Note that the authors explicitly connect the EDX compositional inventory to subsequent interpretation of magnetic and transport data by stating that the relatively small volume fraction of Hg-containing plate-like grains limits the ability to perform precise bulk crystallographic measurements and complicates definitive phase-resolved attribution of superconducting signals.

conclusion.title: Sample phase composition determined by EDX and phase fractions

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometry and bulk characterization indicate the annealed black material is a multiphase composite with approximate volume fractions: $\sim30\%$ $BaCuO_{2}$, $\approx30\%$ $CuO$, $\sim15\%$ of an unidentified oxide containing Hg, Ca and Cu, $<5\%$ of an oxide with Ca and Cu, $\sim5\%$ impurities of unspecified composition, and $\sim15\%$ of the total volume consisting of plate-like Hg–Ba–Ca–Cu-containing grains (the grains that HRTEM/SAED associate with Hg-1223/Hg-1212).

reasoning.step: Treat as established the upstream result "Magnetic detection of superconductivity with onset near $133.5\,$K and multi-phase magnetic signature" (conclusion id="1"), which provides that an oxygen-annealed HBCCO specimen displays superconducting diamagnetism with onset $T_{c}\approx133.5\,$K, a large relative superconducting volume fraction near $125\,$K (FC susceptibility reaching $\sim60\%$ of low-$T$ value), and multiple superconducting features in ZFC at $126\,$K and $112\,$K.

Treat as established the upstream result "Structural identification of Hg-1223, Hg-1212 and a long-period superstructure; lattice constants deduced" (conclusion id="3"), which provides that plate-like grains comprising $\sim15\%$ of the sample volume consist mostly of Hg-1223, disordered Hg-1223/Hg-1212 mixtures, and an ordered superstructure, and that Hg-1223 is the dominant phase seen microscopically in those grains. Fig. 1

Combine these upstream observations: since (a) the magnetic data indicate a dominant superconducting phase whose diamagnetic onset is $T_{c}\approx133.5\,$K and which dominates the magnetic signal near 125 K, and (b) the microscopic structural study shows that Hg-1223 is dominant among the plate-like Hg-containing grains investigated, infer that it is plausible to assign the $\sim133\,$K superconducting phase to Hg-1223. Fig. 2 Fig. 1

Explicitly state the authors' caution: this assignment is presented as a suggested, plausible interpretation rather than as a definitive identification, because the sample is multiphase (only $\sim15\%$ by volume are the Hg-containing plate-like grains), single-phase material was not available, and no phase-resolved transport or magnetic measurements tying a single crystallographic phase to the superconducting transition were performed; therefore there is no unambiguous proof linking the $\sim133\,$K superconductivity to Hg-1223.

Record the authors' contextual analogy and heuristic support for the suggestion: by analogy with thallium- and bismuth-based multi-CuO$_{2}$ layered cuprates (the authors cite prior work on these systems), where transition temperature $T_{c}$ tends to increase with the number of CuO$_{2}$ layers per unit cell, it is consistent with expectations that the three-layer Hg-1223 phase could exhibit a higher $T_{c}$ than single- or two-layer Hg-based phases; the authors therefore present Hg-1223 as the most plausible candidate for the $\sim133\,$K phase while acknowledging the lack of definitive proof. [5]

Reiterate the explicit scientific framing the authors provide: the suggested attribution of the $\sim133\,$K superconductivity to Hg-1223 is a hypothesis pending phase-pure synthesis and phase-resolved measurements, and the authors emphasize that definitive confirmation requires synthesis of single-phase Hg-1223 material and direct phase-resolved correlation of structure, composition and superconducting properties.

conclusion.title: Suggested assignment of the $\sim$133 K superconducting phase to Hg-1223 (hypothesis, not proven)

conclusion_reasoning.conclusion: Based on the dominance of Hg-1223 observed in the grains investigated microscopically and the large relative superconducting volume fraction inferred near $125\,$K, it is suggested (without unambiguous proof) that $HgBa_{2}Ca_{2}Cu_{3}O_{8+x}$ (Hg-1223) is responsible for superconductivity with onset near $\sim133\,$K in these samples. This attribution is explicitly presented as a plausible interpretation rather than as a definitive identification.
